title: Agricultural Adjustment Act of 1933
summary: Created the first major New Deal farm-support framework by paying landowners to reduce production and stabilize agricultural prices during the Depression.
outcome_summary: Provided large-scale federal support to agriculture, but local landlord-centered administration and acreage reduction policies often bypassed or displaced Black tenant farmers and sharecroppers.
impact_notes: A major New Deal intervention whose benefits were real for agricultural producers in general, but whose design and administration often worsened insecurity for Black rural laborers and tenants.

scores.notes: A foundational farm-support law whose unequal local administration makes it an important exclusion-era case.

sources.notes: USDA historical page identifying the Agricultural Adjustment Act as the first major New Deal farm bill.
USDA Equity Commission report noting that Agricultural Adjustment Act policies led to the displacement of many Black tenant farmers and sharecroppers.

title: Civilian Conservation Corps
summary: Created a large federal work-relief and conservation program for unemployed young men during the Great Depression, placing enrollees in camps to perform environmental and infrastructure work.
outcome_summary: Provided jobs and training to many participants, including Black enrollees, but operated within a segregated structure that sharply limited equal treatment and opportunity.
impact_notes: A major relief program that cannot be read as simply exclusionary or simply beneficial because Black participation existed but was mediated by segregation and unequal camp conditions.

scores.notes: A large New Deal jobs program with real access for Black participants but under clearly segregated conditions.

sources.notes: National Park Service overview of the CCC's basic structure and scale.

sources.source_title: Company 818 and Segregation in the Civilian Conservation Corps
sources.notes: National Park Service article documenting how Black CCC participants worked within segregated camps and discriminatory conditions.

title: Works Progress Administration
summary: Established the largest New Deal work-relief program, employing millions of unemployed people in public works, cultural projects, and local infrastructure efforts.
outcome_summary: Offered jobs and public investment on a huge scale, including work performed by Black Americans, but local administration and segregation limited equal access and shaped who benefited most from the program.
impact_notes: An important example of uneven New Deal inclusion: the WPA opened meaningful employment channels for many Black workers while still operating inside discriminatory local systems.

scores.notes: A massive work-relief program with major real benefits but uneven and discriminatory implementation.

sources.notes: Archived White House history page noting Roosevelt's announcement of the Works Progress Administration on May 6, 1935.
National Park Service article describing the WPA as one of the largest and most successful New Deal work-relief programs.

sources.source_title: Claiming Citizenship: African Americans and Works Progress Administration
sources.notes: National Park Service exhibition page focused on African American life, labor, and citizenship claims under WPA-era conditions.
